region_of_origin: Southeast Asia (Papua New Guinea origin; widely cultivated in Singapore, Malaysia, and tropical Asia)

ontology.foodex2_description: Winged bean — raw whole pod; FoodEx2 category: Legume vegetables, raw

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw four-angled bean is approximately 100 g (about 1 cup of chopped pods), commonly eaten as a salad ingredient or blanched side vegetable.

nutrient_source.title: Four-angled bean (Winged bean), raw — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Four-angled bean (Winged bean), raw (cr_id F09020015). Secondary cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #11737 (Winged beans, immature seeds, raw) for saturated fat, sugar, dietary fibre, and micronutrient gap-filling where HPB does not report the field.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for raw four-angled bean (winged bean) pods in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 28 is estimated by analogy to green beans / immature legume pods (GI ~28–32, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (28 × 4.31) / 100 ≈ 1.2 per 100 g serving. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with the food's high protein (6.95 g), high fibre (4.0 g), and low available carbohydrate (4.31 g) profile.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100 g) and high potassium (223 mg/100 g) yield a strongly favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH diet targets.
hypertension.guidance: Raw four-angled bean is an excellent choice for hypertension management; patients should prepare it with minimal added salt or soy sauce to preserve its naturally low-sodium, high-potassium benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate (4.31 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (4.0 g/100 g), and high protein (6.95 g/100 g) combine to produce a low estimated glycemic index (~28) and minimal glycemic load (~1.2 per 100 g).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Four-angled bean is highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes meal planning as a high-protein, high-fibre, low-GI vegetable; patients should avoid cooking it with high-sugar sauces or oyster sauce that would raise the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.12 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, high dietary fibre (4.0 g/100 g), and high potassium (223 mg/100 g) collectively support a cardioprotective dietary pattern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Four-angled bean is cardioprotective and suitable for cardiovascular disease prevention; it should be prepared by steaming, blanching, or light stir-frying with minimal oil to preserve its favourable lipid and fibre profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (4.0 g/100 g exceeding the 3 g anti-inflammatory threshold), vitamin C (18 mg/100 g as antioxidant), calcium (84 mg/100 g), and negligible saturated fat support anti-inflammatory dietary criteria.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Four-angled bean qualifies as an anti-inflammatory food for rheumatoid arthritis management; pairing with turmeric or ginger in stir-fries may further enhance anti-inflammatory benefit, and deep-frying should be avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Four-angled bean, also known as winged bean (Psophocarpus tetragonolobus), is a tropical legume vegetable widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, and across Southeast Asia. The raw pods are characterised by their distinctive four-winged ridges running along the length of the pod, vivid green colour, crisp and crunchy texture, and a mild, slightly sweet flavour reminiscent of green beans. The entire plant is edible — pods, leaves, flowers, and tuberous roots — making it one of the most nutritionally complete legume crops in the tropics. Per 100 g of raw pod, four-angled bean delivers 49 kcal, 6.95 g of protein (exceptionally high for a vegetable), 0.87 g of fat (predominantly polyunsaturated), 4.31 g of available carbohydrate, and 4.0 g of dietary fibre. It is also a meaningful source of calcium (84 mg), potassium (223 mg), iron (1.5 mg), and vitamin C (18 mg). Its sodium content is negligible at 4 mg/100 g. Clinically, four-angled bean is highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low estimated glycemic index (~28), high fibre, and high protein content that slow postprandial glucose absorption. Its very low sodium and high potassium profile aligns with DASH diet principles for hypertension control. The absence of cholesterol and trans fat, combined with high fibre and potassium, makes it cardioprotective. In Singaporean cuisine, it is commonly eaten raw in salads (rojak-style), blanched and served with sambal or peanut sauce, or lightly stir-fried with garlic and minimal oil. It is fully plant-based, vegan, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is four-angled bean good for diabetes?
Is winged bean high in protein?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09020015, confidence 0.95). Secondary micronutrient values (saturated fat, dietary fibre, sugar, vitamins, fatty acid profile) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #11737 (Winged beans, immature seeds, raw) for saturated fat, sugar, dietary fibre, and micronutrient gap-filling where HPB does not report the field. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 (peer-reviewed, University of Sydney GI Database affiliated), clearly documented. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. selenium_mcg set to -1: selenium data for raw four-angled bean is not reported in either the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #11737 as of April 2026. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant-based food; vitamin B12 is naturally absent in all plant foods — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11737. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant-based food with no UV exposure processing; vitamin D is naturally absent — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11737. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): raw legume vegetable; trans fat is naturally absent — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 5. GI value of 28 is an analogous estimate based on green beans / immature legume pods from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for raw winged bean pods exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 6. Dietary fibre (4.0 g/100 g), sugar (1.5 g/100 g), and saturated fat (0.12 g/100 g) values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11737 as HPB Foodo does not report these fields for this entry. 7. The input record classified this food under food_group 'Meat and alternatives' with subgroup 'Legumes' — this classification is preserved as it reflects HPB Singapore's official food grouping convention for high-protein legumes. 8. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input cultural.dietary_tags has been noted but not carried forward as a primary dietary flag, as the food contains 4.31 g carbohydrate per 100 g which may or may not fit individual ketogenic thresholds depending on total daily intake context.

Added saturated_fat_g, dietary_fibre_g, sugar_g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11737 (approved secondary source) to satisfy Q3.

Documented all -1 sentinel values (selenium_mcg) and all confirmed-zero values (vitamin_b12, vitamin_d, trans_fat) in notes per G3 and zero-macro rules.
Added GI analogous estimate (28, low) from Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed source with full estimation basis documented to satisfy Q4.
Added all three required health_context conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance to satisfy Q7.
Expanded natural_language_description to 1,387 characters with food identity, preparation, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and nutrient profile to satisfy Q6.
